titleStmt.title: Letter from Marie Martin to her mother Mary Lewis Martin, 2 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Marie Martin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Marie Martin (1892-1975) to her mother Mary Lewis Martin (n茅e Moore). In this letter Marie thanks her mother for her letter and refers to hearing the sad news of the death of her brother Charlie (1895-1915). Marie gives her mother the details of her day before signing off.Marie Helena Martin later known as 'Mother Mary', was a volunteer nurse during the First World War. She served in field hospitals in Malta, Leeds, and France working for the British army and later went on to become the founder of the Medical Missionaries of Mary (MMM). She returned home briefly in 1916 following the death of her brother Charlie in battle until be called to serve again two months later in a field hospital in Heraldo France.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
25 General Hospital B. E. F. 2ndJuly 1916 My very dearest Mother芒聙聰 A thousand thanks for your
very long letter, I really got quite a
shock about poor Charlie although
I had practically given up hope of
him turning up I still thought
there might be a chance & really
was hoping against hope. as you say
when one realizes it is really
a great consolation to know he
did not suffer for long & feel
sure Our Lady took care of him
& that he is out of all suffering
now芒聙聰 What a brave boy he was &
like him to have his wound dressed
& then go on again. It is really
impossible to realize we shall never
see his dear face again, how we shall
all miss him 芒聙聰 all the poor Mothers the
anxiety these days, until it really
like this touches oneself so much it
brings this terrible war home to us.
I do hope it will end soon every one
has had queit enough sorrow & trouble
I have been very busy these
last few days 芒聙聰On Friday we
evacuated at 11 O芒聙聶ck except for one
patient got all beds made up in
morning, in the afternoon I was on
duty alone with two orderlies
had just finish scrubbing my lockers
& making up new kits when
a convoy came in & before 5 O芒聙聶ck
we were full up 芒聙聰 We had a new
Sister on the block who knew nothing
about Military ways & as she would
have been alone I went back so
had a fine days work most of
them are being cleared out again
to芒聙聰morrow, so I suppose we shall
only just get time to clear up
again for a new batch芒聙聰
I think I had better end there
is a great deal I want to say
but it is 11芒聙聰-30 & lights are suppose
to be out 10芒聙聰-30芒聙聰 I shall write
to芒聙聰morrow all the news & answer
any question you asked芒聙聰
With my very fondest love to
you dearest Mother and although
I am so far away I am always
with you in spirit芒聙聰
Hoping you have had good
news from Tommy芒聙聰 A fat X & heap of Love
Good芒聙聰night Marie
